entry.title: Telegram restrictions, GhostTree evasion, Rokarolla Android malware, and rising imposter scams
entry.content:
This digest reflects a threat landscape shaped by three converging pressures: platform-level controls imposed in response to fraud and abuse, continued innovation in malware stealth and mobile banking theft, and the growing financial impact of large-scale social engineering. Across the reporting, defenders are seeing both technical tradecraft evolution and policy responses that shift risk onto users and platforms. A common thread is trust abuse. Attackers are exploiting familiar channels such as Telegram, social media, Android app lures, SMS, and clipboard workflows, while governments and regulators are responding with broad restrictions and identity-verification proposals that carry their own privacy and enforcement concerns. India temporarily blocked Telegram nationwide ahead of the rerun of the NEET-UG medical entrance exam and also required the service to disable message editing in the country for a limited period. Authorities said scammers had used Telegram channels, groups, and bots to falsely claim they had advance access to exam papers, prompting takedowns despite the agency stating that no paper was actually leaked. The move underscores how encrypted and high-reach messaging platforms continue to be treated as fraud infrastructure during high-stakes public events. In the UK, proposed rules to bar under-16s from social media would likely require age verification during account creation through ID submission or facial age scans. The plan would reportedly extend beyond traditional social media to additional services and features, including livestreaming and stranger contact on gaming platforms. Critics cited in the reporting argue the model is both easy to bypass and likely to create new privacy and identity-theft risks by centralizing sensitive identity and biometric data. The FTC's latest fraud figures add financial context to this broader platform-abuse trend. Americans reportedly lost $3.5 billion to imposter scams in 2025, with more than $2.1 billion of those losses traced to social media; Facebook alone accounted for more losses than text and email combined. Bank security impersonation remained especially costly, while business and government impersonation scams each drove close to or above $1 billion in losses. Researchers described GhostTree, a Windows evasion technique that abuses recursive NTFS junctions to generate effectively limitless valid paths to the same file. A simpler variant, GhostBranch, loops a child junction back to its parent, while GhostTree expands this into a branching structure with an enormous number of possible path combinations. The reporting says the technique can be created by any user with write permissions and does not require administrator privileges. The significance of GhostTree is less the filesystem trick itself than what it says about defensive blind spots. Researchers tested the method against Windows Defender and reported it to Microsoft, with the issue later patched after an initial position that the behavior did not cross a security boundary. For detection engineers, the case illustrates how path parsing and file resolution edge cases remain a viable avenue for malware concealment and scanner bypass. Zimperium detailed a new Android banking trojan, Rokarolla, targeting 217 banking and cryptocurrency applications and supporting 137 remote commands. The malware is delivered through malicious websites masquerading as popular apps such as TikTok and Chrome, using a dropper disguised as Google Play Protect to install the payload and obtain Accessibility permissions. That installation chain reflects the continued effectiveness of fake security prompts and sideloading lures in mobile malware operations. Once installed, Rokarolla uses downloaded HTML overlays to impersonate legitimate banking and wallet applications and harvest credentials, payment card data, and even device unlock secrets such as PINs and patterns. The malware can read and send SMS, intercept calls by making itself the default communications app, scrape notifications and contacts, log keystrokes and screen activity, and replace copied cryptocurrency wallet addresses in the clipboard. The article also notes resilient command-and-control design, including multiple fallback domains, dynamic domain updates, and the ability to disable Google Play Protect after compromise.

New Rokarolla Android Malware Steals PINs, SMS Codes, and Crypto Wallet Funds — Must Read

Zimperium researchers found a new Android banking trojan called Rokarolla that can take over phones by abusing Accessibility, stealing lock-screen PINs, SMS codes, banking credentials, and even redirecting crypto payments. It spreads through fake app sites, disables Play Protect, and uses overlays plus broad remote-control features to target hundreds of banking and wallet apps.

  • Zimperium's zLabs documented a new Android banking trojan called Rokarolla that targets 217 banking and cryptocurrency apps and includes 137 remote commands.
  • Rokarolla spreads through malicious websites impersonating apps such as TikTok and Chrome, using a dropper disguised as Google Play Protect to install the payload and obtain Accessibility access.
  • The malware uses downloaded HTML overlays to mimic legitimate banking and wallet apps, capturing credentials, card details, and even lock-screen PINs, patterns, or passwords.
  • Rokarolla can read and send SMS, make itself the default app for texts and calls to block bank warning calls, log keystrokes and screens, scrape contacts and notifications, and silently replace copied crypto wallet addresses in the clipboard.
  • According to the article, Rokarolla uses multiple fallback command-and-control domains, can receive new domains dynamically, and can disable Google Play Protect after infection.

FTC warns of record $3.5 billion losses to imposter scams in 2025 — Should Read

The FTC says Americans lost a record $3.5 billion to imposter scams in 2025, making them the most reported fraud category and showing losses have nearly tripled since 2020. Social media, especially Facebook, was the biggest contact channel, and the agency says it is increasing enforcement against business and government impersonation schemes.

  • The FTC said Americans reported losing $3.5 billion to imposter scams in 2025, with losses nearly tripling since 2020.
  • Imposter scams were the most reported fraud category in 2025, and the costliest schemes often used fake bank security alerts to convince victims to transfer money.
  • Victims lost nearly $1 billion to business impersonators and about $920 million to government impersonators, according to the FTC.
  • The FTC said more than $2.1 billion in 2025 imposter scam losses were traced to social media, with Facebook accounting for more losses than text and email combined.
  • Since the FTC's Impersonation Rule took effect in April 2024, the agency said it has brought a dozen enforcement actions and secured more than $70 million in consumer redress.

India temporarily blocks Telegram over medical exam cheating fears — May Read

India temporarily blocked Telegram and disabled its message-editing feature ahead of the re-run NEET-UG medical entrance exam to curb cheating scams and rumors about leaked papers. Critics say the move inconveniences legitimate users and does little to fix the underlying exam security problems.

  • India temporarily blocked access to Telegram nationwide until June 22 ahead of the rerun of the NEET-UG medical entrance exam, according to the National Testing Agency.
  • Indian authorities also directed Telegram to disable its message-editing feature in India until June 30 because scammers had used it to make false claims that they leaked exam questions in advance.
  • More than 2 million candidates are scheduled to retake the June 21 exam after the original May test was scrapped following allegations that question papers had been leaked.
  • The National Testing Agency said cyber authorities removed many Telegram channels, groups, and bots advertising fraudulent access to leaked exam papers, even though no exam paper had actually been leaked.
  • Digital rights advocates, including the Internet Freedom Foundation, criticized the restriction as reactive and ineffective and said it would punish ordinary users while failing to address vulnerabilities in the examination system.

GhostTree Attack Abused Recursive Windows Junctions to Hide Malware — May Read

Researchers showed that attackers can abuse recursive NTFS junctions in Windows, a technique dubbed GhostTree, to create effectively endless file paths that can cause recursive scans to hang and leave malware in the folder uninspected. The issue was demonstrated against Windows Defender and highlights a blind spot in file-scanning defenses.

  • The article describes GhostTree as a technique that uses recursive NTFS junctions in Windows to create effectively infinite valid file paths within a folder structure.
  • It states that any user with write access to a folder can create junctions without administrator privileges by using commands such as mklink /J.
  • The simpler GhostBranch method creates a child junction that points back to its parent directory, producing many different valid paths to the same file until Windows path length limits are reached.
  • GhostTree expands this by creating multiple child junctions that each point back to the parent, allowing a branching set of paths that the article says can reach about 2^126 possible path combinations.
  • The researchers say they tested the technique against Windows Defender, reported it to Microsoft, and that the issue was later patched after Microsoft initially said bypassing Defender was not crossing a security boundary.

UK to require ID or face scan before you can make social media accounts — May Read

The UK plans to require new social media users to prove they are over 16—likely with an ID upload or facial age scan—as part of a ban on under-16s creating accounts starting in 2027. Critics warn the system is easy to bypass with VPNs while creating major privacy and data-breach risks for adults and children alike.

  • The UK government plans to ban under-16s from social media, with regulations due before Christmas and the rules set to take effect in spring 2027.
  • To enforce the ban, new account creation will likely require users to prove they are over 16 through an ID upload or a facial age scan.
  • The rules are modeled on Australia’s social media age restrictions, but the UK says it will go further by also restricting features such as livestreaming and stranger contact across additional services including gaming platforms like Roblox.
  • The article states that the system can be bypassed with a VPN, and cites research showing that more than 60% of Australian children were still using social media months after Australia’s ban took effect.
  • Security and privacy experts quoted in the article warn that age-verification systems are weak against motivated minors and create risks of identity theft, blackmail, and data leaks when users must submit IDs or biometric data.
entry.summary: This digest reflects a threat landscape shaped by three converging pressures: platform-level controls imposed in response to fraud and abuse, continued innovation in malware stealth and mobile banking theft, and the growing financial impact of large-scale social engineering. Across the reporting,...

entry.title: THORChain Vault Theft and Funnel Builder WooCommerce Skimming Exploitation
entry.content: This digest covers two financially motivated security events affecting cryptocurrency infrastructure and e-commerce websites. The reporting highlights continued attacker focus on high-value transaction environments, including decentralized finance platforms and WordPress-based checkout flows where compromise can directly enable theft of funds or payment data. THORChain reported that more than $10 million was stolen after one of the platform’s six vaults was compromised. The platform halted trading after detecting abnormal behavior, and officials said initial indications suggested user funds were safe, with the loss limited to protocol-owned funds. THORChain estimated the theft at approximately $10.7 million. According to the report, the network automatically detected the anomalous activity and halted signing operations, which alerted the broader community and prevented additional outbound transactions. The incident fits into a broader pattern of major cryptocurrency platform thefts reported in 2026, including several incidents with losses exceeding $200 million. The case underscores the continued exposure of decentralized finance protocols to attacks against vault, signing, and transaction-control mechanisms. Separately, attackers are actively exploiting a critical unauthenticated vulnerability in the Funnel Builder WordPress plugin to inject malicious JavaScript into WooCommerce checkout pages. The flaw affects all Funnel Builder versions before 3.15.0.3 and had not received an official vulnerability identifier at the time of reporting. The exploitation enables modification of the plugin’s global settings through a publicly exposed checkout endpoint. Sansec observed injected payloads disguised as fake Google Tag Manager or Google Analytics scripts. The malicious JavaScript opens a WebSocket connection to an external location, consistent with payment-card skimming activity against checkout environments. Attackers abuse the plugin’s “External Scripts” setting to add arbitrary JavaScript, placing the malicious code directly in the transaction path used by online shoppers. FunnelKit fixed the issue in Funnel Builder version 3.15.0.3, and the reporting notes that site administrators were advised to inspect the plugin’s checkout external-script settings for rogue entries. The incident reflects persistent targeting of the WordPress and WooCommerce ecosystem, where widely deployed plugins can create large-scale opportunities for payment-data theft when unauthenticated configuration paths are exposed.
entry.summary: This digest covers two financially motivated security events affecting cryptocurrency infrastructure and e-commerce websites. The reporting highlights continued attacker focus on high-value transaction environments, including decentralized finance platforms and WordPress-based checkout flows where...

entry.title: Cisco SD-WAN CVE-2026-20182, Exchange CVE-2026-42897, and npm Supply Chain Attacks
entry.content: This digest is dominated by active exploitation of enterprise infrastructure flaws, developer ecosystem compromise, and continued evolution in credential and session theft tooling. Microsoft Exchange and Cisco SD-WAN both feature prominently, while npm/PyPI incidents show software supply chain attacks continuing to affect high-value technology organizations and developer environments. Microsoft disclosed active exploitation of CVE-2026-42897, a high-severity on-premises Exchange Server vulnerability affecting Exchange Server 2016, Exchange Server 2019, and Exchange Server Subscription Edition. The flaw is described as a cross-site scripting issue that can allow arbitrary JavaScript execution in a user’s browser context when a crafted email is opened in Outlook Web Access under certain interaction conditions. Exchange Online is not affected, and Microsoft is providing temporary mitigation through the Exchange Emergency Mitigation Service while preparing a permanent fix. CISA added Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Controller CVE-2026-20182 to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog and ordered federal agencies to remediate it by May 17, 2026. Cisco rated the authentication bypass vulnerability 10.0 and said it can allow unauthenticated remote attackers to obtain administrative privileges. Cisco attributed exploitation with high confidence to UAT-8616, which attempted to add SSH keys, alter NETCONF configurations, and escalate privileges, while other Cisco SD-WAN flaws have reportedly been chained to deploy web shells and run arbitrary bash commands. The popular npm package node-ipc was compromised through malicious versions 9.1.6, 9.2.3, and 12.0.1. The injected malware executes when applications load, fingerprints hosts, collects environment variables and sensitive files, and exfiltrates compressed data using DNS TXT queries. Reported targets include cloud credentials, SSH keys, Kubernetes and Docker credentials, npm and GitHub tokens, CI/CD secrets, .env files, shell histories, browser profile data, and Microsoft Teams local storage paths. OpenAI disclosed that two employee devices were affected through the Mini Shai-Hulud supply chain attack on TanStack, with limited credential material transferred from affected internal source code repositories. The company said no user data, production systems, or intellectual property were compromised or modified without authorization, but signing certificates for several products were present in impacted repositories and were replaced, requiring macOS updates for affected OpenAI applications. Mistral AI also confirmed a related supply chain incident involving trojanized npm and PyPI SDKs and later faced claims from TeamPCP that nearly 450 repositories were being offered for sale, though Mistral said core repositories and hosted services were not compromised. Flare’s analysis of REMUS underground activity described a malware-as-a-service operation evolving from basic promotion into a structured ecosystem with updates, customer support, log management, and operational refinements. REMUS increasingly emphasized session theft through cookie collection, token handling, browser session restoration, proxy-assisted access continuity, and collection related to password managers such as Bitwarden, 1Password, and LastPass. Unit 42 reported a new Gremlin stealer variant that hides its payload in the .NET Resource section using XOR encoding and sends stolen data to attacker-controlled infrastructure at 194.87.92.109. The variant bundles browser cookies, session tokens, clipboard contents, cryptocurrency wallet data, and FTP and VPN credentials into ZIP archives named after the victim’s public IP address. Expanded capabilities include Discord token theft, cryptocurrency clipboard hijacking, and WebSocket-based hijacking of active browser sessions. Microsoft also reversed course on Edge password handling after research showed the browser decrypted saved credentials at startup and retained them in process memory. A proof-of-concept demonstrated that attackers with Administrator privileges could dump passwords from other users’ Edge processes, prompting Microsoft to introduce a defense-in-depth change that stops loading cleartext passwords into memory at startup. Microsoft reported that Turla has transformed the Kazuar backdoor into a modular peer-to-peer botnet for stealthy persistence. The new architecture uses Kernel, Bridge, and Worker modules to coordinate tasks, proxy command-and-control traffic, and collect data such as keystrokes, system information, file listings, and MAPI details. Kazuar distribution has relied on droppers including Pelmeni and ShadowLoader, with communications supported through Windows Messaging, Mailslot, named pipes, Exchange Web Services, HTTP, and WebSockets. Pwn2Own Berlin 2026 continued to surface zero-day vulnerabilities across major platforms, including Microsoft Exchange, Windows 11, Red Hat Enterprise Linux for Workstations, Cursor, and OpenAI Codex. Orange Tsai of DEVCORE earned $200,000 by chaining three bugs for remote code execution with SYSTEM privileges on Microsoft Exchange. Separately, researchers disclosed the four-vulnerability OpenClaw “Claw Chain,” which could enable data theft, privilege escalation, and persistence, with fixes released in OpenClaw version 2026.4.22. WordPress ecosystem risk was also highlighted by two Avada Builder flaws affecting a plugin with an estimated one million active installations. CVE-2026-4782 allows authenticated arbitrary file read and can expose wp-config.php, while CVE-2026-4798 is an unauthenticated time-based blind SQL injection under specific WooCommerce-related conditions. Microsoft separately described Cloud-Initiated Driver Recovery, a Windows mechanism intended to remotely roll back problematic drivers delivered through Windows Update.
entry.summary: This digest is dominated by active exploitation of enterprise infrastructure flaws, developer ecosystem compromise, and continued evolution in credential and session theft tooling. Microsoft Exchange and Cisco SD-WAN both feature prominently, while npm/PyPI incidents show software supply chain...

entry.title: Cisco SD-WAN CVE-2026-20182 and TeamPCP Supply-Chain Attacks Dominate
entry.content: This digest window is defined by two dominant operational realities: active exploitation of perimeter and infrastructure software, and continued expansion of software supply-chain compromise into developer ecosystems and AI-adjacent organizations. Across the reporting, defenders are also seeing a parallel shift toward session theft, credential harvesting, and abuse of trusted tools rather than noisy malware alone. Cisco SD-WAN CVE-2026-20182 was the highest-priority network infrastructure issue in this cycle. Multiple reports describe the flaw as a critical unauthenticated authentication bypass in Catalyst SD-WAN Controller and Manager, with CISA adding it to KEV and ordering federal agencies to patch on an accelerated deadline. Reporting ties exploitation to threat cluster UAT-8616 and other actors using the bug to gain administrative access, manipulate SD-WAN fabric configuration, register rogue peers, add SSH keys, and escalate privileges. Talos and other coverage indicate broader activity against Cisco SD-WAN environments beyond a single CVE, with additional clusters exploiting related flaws since March 2026 to deploy web shells, Sliver, AdaptixC2, XMRig, credential stealers, and scanning utilities. The pattern suggests sustained post-compromise operations aimed at persistence and network expansion rather than opportunistic one-off access. Microsoft also disclosed active exploitation of CVE-2026-42897, an on-premises Exchange Server cross-site scripting issue affecting Exchange 2016, 2019, and Subscription Edition. The flaw can be triggered through crafted email content viewed in Outlook Web Access, reinforcing that legacy on-prem collaboration infrastructure remains a live target while Exchange Online is not affected. The TeamPCP-linked "Mini Shai-Hulud" campaign continued to ripple outward through the TanStack ecosystem. OpenAI confirmed that two employee devices were compromised, with limited credential exfiltration and no evidence of impact to production systems, customer data, or intellectual property; Mistral AI also reported a developer-device compromise and trojanized SDK releases. TeamPCP is now advertising access to hundreds of Mistral AI repositories for sale, extending the incident from malware delivery into extortion and data monetization. The same reporting highlights how supply-chain compromises are increasingly self-propagating and ecosystem-aware. The TanStack incident affected dozens to hundreds of npm-associated packages tied to organizations including UiPath, OpenSearch, and Guardrails AI, while one Linux payload reportedly included destructive region-gated behavior targeting systems geolocated to Israel or Iran. Separately, TeamPCP was also described as partnering with Breached to gamify package compromise through a public competition around the Shai-Hulud worm. A separate npm incident involving node-ipc showed the same tradecraft trend from a different actor path: malicious versions 9.1.6, 9.2.3, and 12.0.1 embedded credential-harvesting code directly in the CommonJS bundle rather than relying on install hooks. Researchers said the payload harvested roughly 90 categories of secrets, including cloud credentials, SSH material, and database access, and exfiltrated via HTTPS and DNS, with one variant selectively gated to specific targets. Infostealer reporting shows a strong shift from password theft alone to session-centric access theft. REMUS, a fast-evolving malware-as-a-service offering, added browser session theft, SOCKS5 proxying, and support for password managers including 1Password, LastPass, and Bitwarden within months of emergence. Its development cadence reportedly resembled a commercial software product, underscoring how professionally managed stealer ecosystems are adapting to MFA-heavy environments. Gremlin Stealer reflects the same trend at the technical implementation layer. New variants hide payloads in .NET resource sections with XOR encoding, add Discord token theft, cryptocurrency clipboard hijacking, and WebSocket-based browser session hijacking, and use packing and obfuscation to evade static analysis. Together, REMUS and Gremlin illustrate how session tokens, cookies, and authenticated browser state are becoming primary criminal objectives. This shift aligns with broader data on attacker behavior using legitimate tools and native binaries. Bitdefender reported living-off-the-land activity in 84% of 700,000 high-severity incidents, while Cisco Talos emphasized that state-backed actors increasingly rely on valid credentials and trusted internal tooling for long-term access. The common thread across these reports is reduced dependence on bespoke malware where existing access, local tooling, and stolen sessions are sufficient. Several reports focused on product hardening driven by researcher disclosures and exploit discovery. Microsoft said Edge will stop loading all saved passwords into process memory in clear text at startup after public scrutiny of a proof-of-concept that could dump credentials from running processes. OpenClaw also fixed four "Claw Chain" vulnerabilities that researchers said could be chained from sandboxed code execution into data theft, privilege escalation, and persistence. On the vulnerability horizon, Pwn2Own Berlin 2026 produced 24 unique zero-days on the first day alone across products including Windows 11, Microsoft Edge, and Red Hat Linux, highlighting the volume of bug discovery against fully patched targets. Broader commentary from Talos and others points to mounting patch pressure as AI-assisted vulnerability discovery accelerates, while Microsoft announced more centralized control over Windows driver quality through cloud-initiated rollback capabilities.
entry.summary: This digest window is defined by two dominant operational realities: active exploitation of perimeter and infrastructure software, and continued expansion of software supply-chain compromise into developer ecosystems and AI-adjacent organizations. Across the reporting, defenders are also seeing a...

entry.title: REMUS and Gremlin Stealers Evolve; TanStack Supply Chain Attack; Cisco and Exchange Zero-Days
entry.content: This digest covers a six-hour period of significant threat intelligence, highlighting the rapid evolution of infostealers, a major supply chain compromise affecting AI vendors, and critical vulnerabilities requiring immediate attention. Two distinct stealer families—REMUS and Gremlin—demonstrate advanced session theft, MFA bypass, and obfuscation techniques. Meanwhile, the TanStack supply chain attack (attributed to TeamPCP) impacted OpenAI, Mistral AI, and others, forcing certificate revocation and macOS updates. On the vulnerability front, CISA mandated patching of a critical Cisco SD-WAN flaw (CVE-2026-20182) and Microsoft disclosed an exploited Exchange zero-day (CVE-2026-42897). Finally, new research on living-off-the-land binaries underscores the pervasive risk of legitimate tool abuse. Two infostealers have undergone rapid feature expansion. REMUS, a 2026-emerged malware-as-a-service (MaaS), evolved from basic credential theft to include session cookie theft, SOCKS5 proxy support, and dedicated password-manager targeting (1Password, LastPass, Bitwarden). Its operator runs a professional development cycle with versioned updates and customer support. Similarly, Gremlin stealer now hides payloads in .NET Resource sections using XOR encoding and adds modules for Discord token extraction, cryptocurrency clipboard hijacking, and WebSocket-based session hijacking. Both shift toward stealing authenticated browser sessions to bypass MFA. A widespread supply chain attack by the group TeamPCP compromised hundreds of packages associated with TanStack, UiPath, Mistral AI, OpenSearch, and Guardrails AI. OpenAI disclosed that two employee devices were impacted, leading to the revocation of signing certificates for iOS, macOS, and Windows products. macOS users of ChatGPT Desktop, Codex App, Codex CLI, and Atlas must update by June 12, 2026. Mistral AI confirmed a single developer device was affected. The malware delivered on Linux systems includes destructive behavior: a 1-in-6 probability gate (for geolocations of Israel or Iran) triggers audio playback and file deletion, while the malware exits on systems with Russian locale. CISA ordered all federal agencies to patch CVE-2026-20182 in Cisco SD-WAN systems by Sunday. This critical vulnerability (CVSS 10.0) allows unauthenticated remote attackers to gain administrative privileges. Exploitation was observed this month, and prior emergency directives required agencies to identify and log all SD-WAN deployments. Separately, Microsoft warned of CVE-2026-42897, a high-severity Exchange Server cross-site scripting vulnerability that is already exploited in attacks. Microsoft recommends enabling the Exchange Emergency Mitigation Service or applying manual mitigations for air-gapped environments. Patches for Exchange 2016/2019 are limited to Extended Support customers. Bitdefender's 45-day Internal Attack Surface Assessment analyzed 700,000 high-severity incidents and found legitimate tool abuse in 84% of cases. A clean Windows 11 install contains 133 unique living-off-the-land binaries across 987 instances, with PowerShell active on 73% of endpoints. Early-access customers reduced their attack surface by up to 70% by locking down LOLBins and remote tools. Gartner projects that preemptive cybersecurity will account for 50% of IT security spending by 2030, reflecting growing awareness of attack surface reduction. Microsoft introduced Cloud-Initiated Driver Recovery to automatically roll back problematic Windows drivers, addressing quality and security issues. The feature is being tested through August 2026 and will apply to drivers rejected during Flighting or Gradual Rollout starting September 2026. The Driver Quality Initiative (DQI) aims to raise overall driver reliability and security.
entry.summary: This digest covers a six-hour period of significant threat intelligence, highlighting the rapid evolution of infostealers, a major supply chain compromise affecting AI vendors, and critical vulnerabilities requiring immediate attention. Two distinct stealer families—REMUS and Gremlin—demonstrate...

entry.title: Fragnesia, Exim, NGINX Flaws; APT Groups Target Ukraine and South Korea; Dream Market Admin Arrested
entry.content: The past 24 hours have seen a surge in critical vulnerability disclosures affecting widely used software, alongside ongoing geopolitical cyber operations from state-linked threat actors targeting Ukraine and South Korea. Law enforcement arrested the alleged administrator of the Dream Market dark web marketplace, and several ransomware and espionage campaigns were detailed, highlighting the breadth of current threats. Multiple high-severity vulnerabilities have been disclosed, with proof-of-concept exploits already public for some. The Fragnesia flaw (CVE-2026-46300) in the Linux kernel allows unprivileged local attackers to gain root access via page-cache corruption in the XFRM ESP-in-TCP subsystem, affecting all kernels before May 13, 2026. A critical heap buffer overflow in NGINX's rewrite module (CVE-2026-42945), present for 18 years, enables unauthenticated remote code execution. Exim mailer versions 4.97 through 4.99.2 are vulnerable to a use-after-free (CVE-2026-45185) that allows unauthenticated RCE when compiled with GnuTLS. The PraisonAI authentication bypass (CVE-2026-44338) was actively scanned within hours of disclosure. Additionally, two unpatched Windows vulnerabilities—YellowKey (BitLocker bypass) and GreenPlasma (privilege escalation)—have publicly released exploits. Multiple state-linked threat actors are conducting targeted campaigns. The Belarus-aligned Ghostwriter group is deploying geofenced PDF phishing against Ukrainian government entities to deliver PicassoLoader and Cobalt Strike, while the Russia-affiliated Gamaredon group uses spear-phishing with RAR archives exploiting CVE-2025-8088 to drop GammaDrop and GammaLoad. On the opposing side, pro-Ukraine hacktivists BO Team and Head Mare are targeting Russian organizations with ZeronetKit and ZeroSSH backdoor via spear-phishing. Kimsuky has expanded its arsenal with new PebbleDash variants and legitimate tools like VSCode tunneling, targeting South Korean entities and global defense organizations. Iran-linked MuddyWater conducted a campaign against at least nine high-profile organizations, including a major South Korean electronics manufacturer, using DLL sideloading and exfiltrating data via sendit.sh. West Pharmaceutical Services disclosed a cyberattack on May 4, 2026, that resulted in unauthorized data exfiltration and system encryption. The company engaged incident response and has partially restored core systems, but no ransomware group has claimed responsibility, and the full impact remains unknown. The KongTuke group has adopted Microsoft Teams as an attack vector, tricking employees into running a malicious PowerShell command that deploys the ModeloRAT malware, which features resilient C2 infrastructure and SYSTEM-level persistence. Separately, AI hallucinations in language models are creating real security risks, including missed zero-day threats, false positives causing alert fatigue, and incorrect remediation recommendations that can escalate incidents. German authorities arrested Owe Martin Andresen, the alleged main administrator of the Dream Market dark web marketplace, following a US indictment on money laundering charges. Authorities seized approximately $1.7 million in gold bars, cash, and cryptocurrency wallets holding $1.2 million in suspected proceeds. Dream Market facilitated illicit drug sales before its 2019 shutdown. Dell confirmed that version 5.5.16.0 of its SupportAssist Remediation service causes Windows blue-screen crashes; uninstalling the service resolves the issue but may remove system repair points. Microsoft fixed a Windows Autopatch bug that had deployed restricted driver updates on some EU Windows 11 devices despite administrative policies, and also addressed a BitLocker recovery issue affecting Windows 11 systems after April 2026 updates, though Windows 10 and Server users await a fix. The European Commission announced plans to propose the Digital Fairness Act this summer, targeting addictive design practices and restricting teen social media access.
entry.summary: The past 24 hours have seen a surge in critical vulnerability disclosures affecting widely used software, alongside ongoing geopolitical cyber operations from state-linked threat actors targeting Ukraine and South Korea. Law enforcement arrested the alleged administrator of the Dream Market dark...

entry.title: KongTuke, Kimsuky, and MuddyWater Espionage; Critical CVEs in Linux, NGINX, Exim, and BitLocker; West Pharma Breach
entry.content: This digest covers advanced espionage campaigns by KongTuke, Kimsuky, and MuddyWater targeting South Korean entities and corporate networks, alongside critical vulnerabilities in Linux, NGINX, Exim, and BitLocker enabling privilege escalation and remote code execution. Incident reporting includes a ransomware attack on West Pharmaceutical Services and the arrest of a Dream Market administrator, while vendor defects in Dell and Microsoft software introduce stability issues. AI-driven dynamics are highlighted through reports on hallucination risks and Microsoft's MDASH system for autonomous vulnerability discovery. KongTuke now employs Microsoft Teams phishing to trick employees into pasting malicious PowerShell commands that deploy ModeloRAT, featuring resilient command-and-control architecture with automatic failover and Unicode whitespace spoofing. Kimsuky has expanded its arsenal with PebbleDash and AppleSeed malware variants targeting South Korean organizations and defense sectors, utilizing Rust-based backdoors, VSCode tunneling for persistence, and code exhibiting LLM-assisted generation. MuddyWater conducted a cyber-espionage campaign against a major South Korean electronics manufacturer in February 2026, leveraging DLL sideloading and Node.js loaders to steal industrial data before exfiltrating it via sendit.sh. Overlapping distribution methods and target profiles suggest coordinated activity against critical infrastructure and industrial entities. Fragnesia (CVE-2026-46300) is a Linux kernel local privilege escalation flaw with a CVSS score of 7.8 that allows unprivileged attackers to gain root access through deterministic page-cache corruption in the XFRM ESP-in-TCP subsystem. NGINX CVE-2026-42945 enables unauthenticated remote code execution via a heap buffer overflow in the rewrite module triggered by specific PCRE capture directives. Exim CVE-2026-45185 permits remote code execution during TLS shutdown when processing BDAT chunked SMTP traffic on builds compiled with GnuTLS. Researchers released proof-of-concept exploits for BitLocker bypasses YellowKey and GreenPlasma, which grant unrestricted drive access and escalate privileges via memory-section manipulation. PraisonAI CVE-2026-44338 exposes an authentication bypass in legacy Flask APIs, allowing unauthenticated users to enumerate configurations and trigger workflows. West Pharmaceutical Services detected a cyberattack on May 4, 2026, resulting in unauthorized data exfiltration and system encryption, with core enterprise systems partially restored after engaging Palo Alto Networks' Unit 42. Owe Martin Andresen, alleged main administrator of the defunct Dream Market dark web marketplace, was arrested in Germany and indicted in the US on multiple money laundering charges. Authorities recovered gold, cash, and cryptocurrency wallets linked to illicit proceeds from the platform, which facilitated drug sales until its 2019 shutdown. Dell confirmed that version 5.5.16.0 of the SupportAssist Remediation service triggers Windows blue-screen crashes, requiring service disablement or uninstallation to resolve stability issues. Microsoft addressed a BitLocker recovery problem affecting Windows 11 25H2 systems after installing April 2026 security updates, while Windows 10 and Server customers await a future fix for the same issue. A separate Windows Autopatch bug deployed restricted drivers on EU Windows 11 devices despite administrative policies, causing unexpected reboots and system failures resolved via a service-side update. AI hallucinations create cybersecurity risks by generating false positives, missing zero-day threats, and recommending incorrect remediation actions based on flawed training data and prompt ambiguity. Microsoft introduced MDASH, a multi-agent AI system that autonomously discovered 16 vulnerabilities in the latest Patch Tuesday release, including two critical remote code execution flaws. The system utilizes auditor, debater, and prover agents to analyze source code, validate findings, and confirm exploitable issues within complex codebases.
entry.summary: This digest covers advanced espionage campaigns by KongTuke, Kimsuky, and MuddyWater targeting South Korean entities and corporate networks, alongside critical vulnerabilities in Linux, NGINX, Exim, and BitLocker enabling privilege escalation and remote code execution. Incident reporting includes a...
